1:
"And I looked, and, lo, a Lamb stood on the mount Sion, and with him an hundred forty and four thousand, having his Father's name written in their foreheads." The witnesses note the Lamb on Mount Zion with the 144,000. On "a Lamb stood on the mount Sion," the grammatical voices note the contrast with the beast. Barnes and Robertson note the scene: "a Lamb stood on the mount Sion, and with him an hundred forty and four thousand" — over against the beast and its worshippers (ch. 13), the Lamb (Christ) is seen standing on Mount Zion with his redeemed company; the vision turns from the persecuting power to the triumphant Lamb and his faithful; Mount Zion signifying the church, the place of God's people, or the heavenly Zion.

On "an hundred forty and four thousand," the witnesses note the redeemed company. Barnes and Ellicott note that the "144,000" (the sealed of 7:4) are the Lamb's faithful company — whether the same sealed multitude or the complete number of the redeemed; the symbolic number marks the full, complete company of God's people, preserved through the beast's persecution and gathered with the Lamb.

On "having his Father's name written in their foreheads," the latin-fathers Victorinus and Bede and the grammatical-historical note the seal: the company has "his Father's name written in their foreheads" — the mark of God's ownership (the seal of 7:3), in pointed contrast to the mark of the beast (13:16); they belong to God and the Lamb, openly sealed as his; the Father's name on their foreheads marks them as his redeemed. The verse displays the Lamb on Mount Zion with the 144,000.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the scene — "a Lamb stood on the mount Sion, and with him an hundred forty and four thousand," the triumphant Lamb with his redeemed company, over against the beast (Barnes, Robertson); the "144,000" the sealed, complete company of the redeemed (7:4, Barnes, Ellicott); "his Father's name written in their foreheads," the seal of God's ownership, contrasting the beast's mark (7:3; 13:16, Victorinus, Bede). The verse displays the Lamb's company. The verse marks the Lamb: on Zion. The verse displays the company: 144,000. The verse displays the seal: the Father's name. The verse displays the contrast: with the beast's mark. The vision displays the redeemed sealed with God's name. The verse displays the Lamb: standing. The verse displays the company: sealed. The verse displays the name: the Father's. The Lamb on Mount Zion with the 144,000 displays the triumphant counterpart to the beast: the Lamb stands on Mount Zion with his 144,000, who bear the Father's name on their foreheads — the redeemed company, sealed as God's own, over against the beast and its marked worshippers. The verse displays the sealed company, singing the new song (vv.2-3).
2:
"And I heard a voice from heaven, as the voice of many waters, and as the voice of a great thunder: and I heard the voice of harpers harping with their harps:" The brief verse notes the great voice from heaven and the harpers. The grammatical voices note the mighty sound. Barnes and Robertson note the heavenly music: "I heard a voice from heaven, as the voice of many waters, and as the voice of a great thunder: and I heard the voice of harpers harping with their harps" — a great voice from heaven, like the roar of many waters and a great thunder, yet also like the sweet sound of harpers playing; the combination of majesty (waters, thunder) and melody (harps) in the heavenly song.

On "as the voice of many waters, and as the voice of a great thunder," the witnesses note the majesty. Barnes and Ellicott note that the voice "as many waters... a great thunder" marks the volume, power, and majesty of the heavenly sound — overwhelming and grand; the vast company's song is mighty as the sea and the storm.

On "the voice of harpers harping with their harps," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the melody: the voice was also "as harpers harping with their harps" — the sound, though mighty, was also musical and sweet, like skilled harpers; the heavenly worship combines power and beauty; the redeemed sing to the accompaniment of harps (cf. 5:8; 15:2). The verse displays the great voice from heaven and the harpers.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the sound — "a voice from heaven, as the voice of many waters, and as the voice of a great thunder: and... the voice of harpers harping with their harps," the heavenly song mighty as waters and thunder yet melodious as harps (Barnes, Robertson); "as many waters... a great thunder" the majesty and power of the sound (Barnes, Ellicott); "harpers harping with their harps," the musical sweetness of the heavenly worship (5:8, Cyril). The verse displays the heavenly song. The verse marks the voice: from heaven. The verse displays the majesty: waters and thunder. The verse displays the melody: the harps. The verse displays the worship: mighty and sweet. The sound displays the heavenly song grand and melodious. The verse displays the voice: mighty. The verse displays the harps: harping. The verse displays the song: heavenly. The great voice from heaven and the harpers display the heavenly worship accompanying the Lamb's company: a voice like many waters and a great thunder, yet like harpers harping with their harps — the song of the redeemed, mighty in majesty and sweet in melody. The verse displays the heavenly harpers, singing the new song (v.3).
3:
"And they sung as it were a new song before the throne, and before the four beasts, and the elders: and no man could learn that song but the hundred and forty and four thousand, which were redeemed from the earth." The witnesses note the new song that only the redeemed could learn. On "they sung as it were a new song before the throne," the grammatical voices note the new song. Barnes and Robertson note the worship: "they sung as it were a new song before the throne, and before the four beasts (living creatures), and the elders" — the 144,000 sing a new song before God's throne, the living creatures, and the elders; a "new song" (5:9) befitting the new, fuller experience of redemption; the song of the redeemed, sung in the heavenly court.

On "a new song," the witnesses note its character. Barnes and Ellicott note that the "new song" is the song of redemption — new because it celebrates the new and greater work of grace; the song peculiar to the redeemed, expressing the joy and gratitude of those saved by the Lamb.

On "no man could learn that song but the hundred and forty and four thousand, which were redeemed from the earth," the grammatical-historical note the exclusive song: "no man could learn that song but the 144,000, which were redeemed from the earth" — only the redeemed can learn or sing this song; the experience of redemption is its prerequisite; those who have not been redeemed cannot enter into its meaning or joy; the song belongs uniquely to the ransomed company. The verse displays the new song that only the redeemed could learn.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the song — "they sung as it were a new song before the throne, and before the four beasts, and the elders," the 144,000 singing the new song of redemption in the heavenly court (5:9, Barnes, Robertson); the "new song" the song of redemption, peculiar to the redeemed (Barnes, Ellicott); "no man could learn that song but the 144,000, which were redeemed," the song exclusive to the ransomed, requiring the experience of redemption (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the redeemed's song. The verse marks the song: new. The verse displays the place: before the throne. The verse displays the exclusivity: only the redeemed. The verse displays the company: redeemed from earth. The song displays the redeemed alone able to sing redemption's song. The verse displays the song: new. The verse displays the learning: exclusive. The verse displays the redeemed: singing. The new song that only the redeemed could learn displays the unique worship of the ransomed: the 144,000 sing a new song before the throne, the living creatures, and the elders — a song none could learn but the redeemed from the earth, the song of redemption belonging uniquely to those saved by the Lamb. The verse displays the singing redeemed, described in their purity (vv.4-5).
4:
"These are they which were not defiled with women; for they are virgins. These are they which follow the Lamb whithersoever he goeth. These were redeemed from among men, being the firstfruits unto God and to the Lamb." The witnesses note the purity of the redeemed who follow the Lamb. On "these are they which were not defiled with women; for they are virgins," interpreters note the meaning of the purity. Barnes and Robertson note that "not defiled with women; for they are virgins (parthenoi)" is most often taken figuratively — the chastity is spiritual: the redeemed are undefiled by the spiritual fornication of idolatry and apostasy (the beast-worship, ch. 13), keeping themselves pure for Christ; a minority take it of literal celibacy/virginity, but the dominant reading is spiritual purity from idolatry; the figure of the church as a chaste virgin (2 Corinthians 11:2).

On "these are they which follow the Lamb whithersoever he goeth," the witnesses note their devotion. Barnes and Ellicott note that the redeemed "follow the Lamb whithersoever he goeth" — they are wholly devoted to Christ, following him in all things, in obedience, suffering, and glory; the mark of the true disciple is unreserved following of the Lamb.

On "redeemed from among men, being the firstfruits unto God and to the Lamb," the grammatical-historical note their consecration: they are "redeemed from among men, being the firstfruits unto God and to the Lamb" — ransomed out of mankind and consecrated to God as firstfruits; whether the first and choicest of the redeemed, or the whole harvest consecrated to God; set apart as holy to God and the Lamb. The verse displays the purity of the redeemed who follow the Lamb.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the purity — "not defiled with women; for they are virgins," the redeemed undefiled, chiefly read as spiritual purity from the idolatry of beast-worship (2 Corinthians 11:2, Barnes, Robertson); "follow the Lamb whithersoever he goeth," their unreserved devotion to Christ (Barnes, Ellicott); "redeemed from among men, being the firstfruits unto God," consecrated as holy firstfruits (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the pure followers. The verse marks the purity: undefiled. The verse displays the meaning: chiefly spiritual. The verse displays the devotion: follow the Lamb. The verse displays the consecration: firstfruits. The description displays the redeemed pure, devoted, and consecrated. The verse displays the virgins: undefiled. The verse displays the following: of the Lamb. The verse displays the firstfruits: to God. The purity of the redeemed who follow the Lamb displays the character of the ransomed company: undefiled (chiefly, kept pure from the spiritual fornication of idolatry), following the Lamb wherever he goes, redeemed from among men as firstfruits unto God and the Lamb — pure, devoted, and consecrated. The verse displays the followers of the Lamb, blameless in speech (v.5).
5:
"And in their mouth was found no guile: for they are without fault before the throne of God." The brief verse notes the blamelessness of the redeemed. The grammatical voices note their truthfulness and purity. Barnes and Robertson note the description: "in their mouth was found no guile: for they are without fault before the throne of God" — the redeemed are marked by truthfulness (no guile/falsehood in their mouth) and blamelessness (without fault); they are sincere and pure, fit to stand before God's throne.

On "in their mouth was found no guile," the witnesses note their sincerity. Barnes and Ellicott note that "no guile (no lie, no deceit) in their mouth" marks their truthfulness and integrity — in contrast to the lies of the beast and false prophet (ch. 13); the redeemed are sincere, holding and speaking the truth; the mark of those who belong to the God of truth (cf. Zephaniah 3:13; Psalm 32:2).

On "without fault before the throne of God," the grammatical-historical note their blamelessness: they are "without fault (blameless, faultless) before the throne of God" — accounted blameless in God's presence; whether by their consecrated lives or (more deeply) by the righteousness of the Lamb that makes them faultless before God; the redeemed stand unblemished before the throne, the perfected company of the saved. The verse displays the blamelessness of the redeemed.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the blamelessness — "in their mouth was found no guile: for they are without fault before the throne of God," the redeemed truthful and blameless, fit to stand before God (Barnes, Robertson); "no guile in their mouth" their sincerity and integrity, contrasting the beast's lies (Zephaniah 3:13, Barnes, Ellicott); "without fault before the throne," accounted blameless in God's presence (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the faultless redeemed. The verse marks the mouth: no guile. The verse displays the sincerity: truthful. The verse displays the blamelessness: without fault. The verse displays the standing: before the throne. The description displays the redeemed sincere and faultless before God. The verse displays the guile: absent. The verse displays the fault: none. The verse displays the throne: stood before. The blamelessness of the redeemed displays the close of their description: no guile is found in their mouth, and they are without fault before the throne of God — truthful, sincere, and blameless, the perfected company standing unblemished in God's presence. The verse displays the faultless redeemed, as the first angel proclaims the everlasting gospel (vv.6-7).
6:
"And I saw another angel fly in the midst of heaven, having the everlasting gospel to preach unto them that dwell on the earth, and to every nation, and kindred, and tongue, and people," The witnesses note the angel with the everlasting gospel for all nations. On "another angel fly in the midst of heaven, having the everlasting gospel to preach," the grammatical voices note the first of three angels. Barnes and Robertson note the heavenly herald: "another angel fly in the midst of heaven (mid-heaven, the zenith), having the everlasting gospel (eternal good news) to preach" — an angel flies in mid-heaven, visible to all, bearing the everlasting gospel to proclaim; the first of three angelic messages; the gospel proclaimed in the face of the beast's dominion.

On "the everlasting gospel," the witnesses note its character. Barnes and Henry note that the "everlasting gospel" is the eternal, unchanging good news of God — the abiding message of salvation; its proclamation to all the earth, even in the time of the beast, marks God's continued call to repentance and faith; the gospel endures and is preached to all.

On "to preach unto them that dwell on the earth, and to every nation, and kindred, and tongue, and people," the grammatical-historical note the universal scope: the gospel is proclaimed "to every nation, and kindred, and tongue, and people" — the fourfold designation marks its universal proclamation to all mankind; the everlasting gospel is for the whole world; God's saving message reaches every people, even under the beast's sway. The verse displays the angel with the everlasting gospel for all nations.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the herald — "another angel fly in the midst of heaven, having the everlasting gospel to preach," the angel bearing the eternal gospel to proclaim, the first of three messages (Barnes, Robertson); "the everlasting gospel" the eternal, unchanging good news of salvation (Barnes, Henry); "to every nation, and kindred, and tongue, and people," its universal proclamation to all mankind (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the gospel-angel. The verse marks the angel: in mid-heaven. The verse displays the message: the everlasting gospel. The verse displays the scope: every nation. The verse displays the proclamation: universal. The angel displays the eternal gospel preached to all the world. The verse displays the angel: flying. The verse displays the gospel: everlasting. The verse displays the nations: all. The angel with the everlasting gospel for all nations displays the first of the three angelic messages: an angel flies in mid-heaven bearing the everlasting gospel to preach to every nation, kindred, tongue, and people — God's eternal saving message proclaimed to all the world, even under the beast's dominion. The verse displays the gospel-angel, calling all to fear God (v.7).
7:
"Saying with a loud voice, Fear God, and give glory to him; for the hour of his judgment is come: and worship him that made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and the fountains of waters." The witnesses note the angel's call to fear God, for the hour of judgment is come. On "Fear God, and give glory to him," the grammatical voices note the summons. Barnes and Robertson note the angel's proclamation: "Fear God, and give glory to him" — the everlasting gospel's call is to fear and glorify God; over against the worship of the beast, the angel summons all to reverence and honor the true God; the call to true worship in the face of false.

On "for the hour of his judgment is come," the witnesses note the warning. Barnes and Ellicott note the ground of the summons: "the hour of his judgment is come" — the time of God's judgment has arrived; the call to fear and worship God is urged by the nearness of judgment; the warning that lends urgency to the gospel-summons; men must turn to God, for judgment is at hand.

On "worship him that made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and the fountains of waters," the grammatical-historical note the call to worship the Creator: "worship him that made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and the fountains of waters" — the true object of worship is the Creator of all; over against the beast and its image, men are called to worship the God who made all things; the appeal to God as Creator grounds the duty of worshiping him alone. The verse displays the angel's call to fear God, for the hour of judgment is come.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the summons — "Fear God, and give glory to him," the call to reverence and honor the true God, over against the beast (Barnes, Robertson); "the hour of his judgment is come," the warning of imminent judgment urging the summons (Barnes, Ellicott); "worship him that made heaven, and earth, and the sea," the call to worship the Creator alone (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the gospel-summons. The verse marks the call: fear God. The verse displays the glory: give to him. The verse displays the warning: judgment is come. The verse displays the worship: of the Creator. The proclamation displays all called to fear and worship the true God. The verse displays the fear: urged. The verse displays the judgment: imminent. The verse displays the Creator: worshiped. The angel's call to fear God, for the hour of judgment is come, displays the content of the everlasting gospel's proclamation: fear God and give him glory, for the hour of his judgment has come, and worship the Creator of heaven, earth, sea, and the fountains of waters — the call to true worship of God, urged by the nearness of judgment. The verse displays the call to worship, followed by the fall of Babylon (v.8).
8:
"And there followed another angel, saying, Babylon is fallen, is fallen, that great city, because she made all nations drink of the wine of the wrath of her fornication." The witnesses note the second angel proclaiming Babylon's fall. On "there followed another angel, saying, Babylon is fallen, is fallen, that great city," the grammatical voices note the second message. Barnes and Robertson note the proclamation of doom: "another angel, saying, Babylon is fallen, is fallen, that great city" — the second angel announces the fall of Babylon; the repetition ("is fallen, is fallen") marks the certainty and completeness of her ruin (drawn from Isaiah 21:9; Jeremiah 51:8); "that great city" is the great antichristian power, named Babylon.

On the identity of Babylon, interpreters note the views. Barnes and Gill note that "Babylon" is the symbolic name for the great corrupt and persecuting power (described fully in chs. 17-18); the historicists read it of papal Rome (the great city on seven hills, 17:9), the apostate religious power; others of pagan Rome or the world-system; the name "Babylon" marks her as the seat of idolatry, corruption, and opposition to God's people.

On "she made all nations drink of the wine of the wrath of her fornication," the grammatical-historical note the cause of her fall: Babylon fell "because she made all nations drink of the wine of the wrath of her fornication" — she seduced the nations into her spiritual fornication (idolatry, corruption, false worship), intoxicating them with her corrupting influence; her fall is the judgment for her seduction of the nations. The verse displays the second angel proclaiming Babylon's fall.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the proclamation — "Babylon is fallen, is fallen, that great city," the second angel announcing the certain, complete fall of the great antichristian power (Isaiah 21:9, Barnes, Robertson); "Babylon" the symbolic name for the corrupt persecuting power, papal Rome to historicists (chs. 17-18, Barnes, Gill); "she made all nations drink of the wine of the wrath of her fornication," her seduction of the nations the cause of her fall (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fall of Babylon. The verse marks the angel: the second. The verse displays the doom: Babylon fallen. The verse displays the city: the great. The verse displays the cause: her fornication. The proclamation displays the great corrupt power doomed for seducing the nations. The verse displays the Babylon: fallen. The verse displays the nations: seduced. The verse displays the wine: of fornication. The second angel proclaiming Babylon's fall displays the second of the three messages: another angel announces "Babylon is fallen, is fallen, that great city," for she made all nations drink the wine of her fornication — the great antichristian power doomed for seducing the nations into her corruption. The verse displays the fallen Babylon, before the third angel's warning (vv.9-11).
9:
"And the third angel followed them, saying with a loud voice, If any man worship the beast and his image, and receive his mark in his forehead, or in his hand," The brief verse notes the third angel's warning against worshipping the beast. The grammatical voices note the solemn condition. Barnes and Robertson note the third message: "the third angel followed them, saying with a loud voice, If any man worship the beast and his image, and receive his mark in his forehead, or in his hand" — the third angel utters a solemn warning against beast-worship; the condition is stated (worshiping the beast and image, receiving the mark) before the dreadful consequence (vv.10-11) is declared.

On "worship the beast and his image," the witnesses note the sin warned against. Barnes and Ellicott note that the warning concerns those who "worship the beast and his image" — the idolatry of the antichristian power (ch. 13); the third angel warns against the very worship the beast and false prophet enforce; the choice is set before all: worship the beast, or fear God (v.7).

On "receive his mark in his forehead, or in his hand," the grammatical-historical note the badge of allegiance: receiving "his mark in his forehead, or in his hand" (13:16) marks open allegiance to the beast; the warning is against taking the mark of subjection to the antichristian power; the solemn condition prepares for the warning of wrath against all who so submit. The verse displays the third angel's warning against worshipping the beast.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the warning — "If any man worship the beast and his image, and receive his mark," the third angel's solemn warning against beast-worship (Barnes, Robertson); "worship the beast and his image" the idolatry the beast enforces, against which the choice is set (Barnes, Ellicott); "receive his mark in his forehead, or in his hand," the badge of allegiance to the beast (13:16, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the third angel's warning. The verse marks the angel: the third. The verse displays the condition: worship the beast. The verse displays the image: and his image. The verse displays the mark: forehead or hand. The warning displays the dreadful condition of beast-worship stated. The verse displays the worship: warned against. The verse displays the mark: received. The verse displays the warning: solemn. The third angel's warning against worshipping the beast displays the third of the messages: the angel warns, with a loud voice, against any who worship the beast and his image and receive his mark in forehead or hand — the solemn condition set before the dreadful consequence of wrath. The verse displays the warning's condition, followed by the doom of the beast-worshippers (vv.10-11).
10:
"The same shall drink of the wine of the wrath of God, which is poured out without mixture into the cup of his indignation; and he shall be tormented with fire and brimstone in the presence of the holy angels, and in the presence of the Lamb:" The witnesses note the wrath of God against the worshippers of the beast. On "the same shall drink of the wine of the wrath of God, which is poured out without mixture into the cup of his indignation," the grammatical voices note the unmingled wrath. Barnes and Robertson note the dreadful threat: "the same shall drink of the wine of the wrath of God, which is poured out without mixture (unmingled, full strength) into the cup of his indignation" — the beast-worshipper shall drink God's wrath, poured undiluted into the cup of his indignation; the "without mixture" marks the full, unmitigated severity of the wrath; the figure of the cup of God's anger (Psalm 75:8; Jeremiah 25:15).

On "poured out without mixture," the witnesses note the unmitigated severity. Barnes and Ellicott note that the wine "without mixture" is wrath at full strength, undiluted by mercy; the judgment on the beast-worshipper is unrelieved; the cup of indignation is drunk in its full force.

On "tormented with fire and brimstone in the presence of the holy angels, and in the presence of the Lamb," the grammatical-historical note the torment: the worshipper "shall be tormented with fire and brimstone (the figure of dreadful, unending punishment) in the presence of the holy angels, and in the presence of the Lamb" — the torment is with fire and brimstone (Sodom, Genesis 19; the lake of fire, 20:10), before the holy angels and the Lamb; the punishment is open, witnessed by heaven; the most solemn warning of wrath against beast-worship. The verse displays the wrath of God against the worshippers of the beast.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the wrath — "shall drink of the wine of the wrath of God, which is poured out without mixture," the beast-worshipper drinking God's undiluted wrath (Psalm 75:8, Barnes, Robertson); "without mixture" the full, unmitigated severity, undiluted by mercy (Barnes, Ellicott); "tormented with fire and brimstone in the presence of the holy angels, and... the Lamb," the dreadful, witnessed torment (20:10, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the wrath on the beast-worshippers. The verse marks the wine: of wrath. The verse displays the cup: of indignation. The verse displays the torment: fire and brimstone. The verse displays the witnesses: angels and the Lamb. The warning displays God's full wrath against beast-worship. The verse displays the wrath: undiluted. The verse displays the torment: with fire. The verse displays the presence: of the Lamb. The wrath of God against the worshippers of the beast displays the dreadful warning: whoever worships the beast shall drink the undiluted wine of God's wrath from the cup of his indignation and be tormented with fire and brimstone before the holy angels and the Lamb — the most solemn threat against beast-worship. The verse displays the threatened wrath, the torment ascending forever (v.11).
11:
"And the smoke of their torment ascendeth up for ever and ever: and they have no rest day nor night, who worship the beast and his image, and whosoever receiveth the mark of his name." The brief verse notes the unending torment of the beast-worshippers. The grammatical voices note the perpetual punishment. Barnes and Robertson note the dreadful continuance: "the smoke of their torment ascendeth up for ever and ever: and they have no rest day nor night" — the smoke of their torment rises forever, and they have no rest at all; the figure of perpetual, unceasing punishment (drawn from Isaiah 34:10; cf. Genesis 19:28); the unending and restless torment of those who worship the beast.

On "the smoke of their torment ascendeth up for ever and ever," the witnesses note the perpetuity. Barnes and Ellicott note that "for ever and ever" marks the eternal, ceaseless duration of the punishment; the rising smoke is the perpetual token of the judgment; the most emphatic statement of the endless torment of the impenitent beast-worshipper.

On "they have no rest day nor night, who worship the beast and his image," the grammatical-historical note the restlessness: "they have no rest day nor night" — in pointed contrast to the rest of those who die in the Lord (v.13); the beast-worshippers have no respite from their torment, while the faithful rest from their labors; the warning is set against the blessing to follow. The verse displays the unending torment of the beast-worshippers.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the torment — "the smoke of their torment ascendeth up for ever and ever: and they have no rest day nor night," the perpetual, unceasing punishment of the beast-worshippers (Isaiah 34:10, Barnes, Robertson); "for ever and ever" the eternal duration (Barnes, Ellicott); "no rest day nor night," the restless torment, contrasting the rest of the faithful (v.13, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the endless torment. The verse marks the smoke: ascending forever. The verse displays the duration: for ever and ever. The verse displays the rest: none. The verse displays the contrast: with the faithful. The torment displays the beast-worshippers in perpetual restless punishment. The verse displays the smoke: rising. The verse displays the torment: endless. The verse displays the rest: denied. The unending torment of the beast-worshippers displays the climax of the third angel's warning: the smoke of their torment rises forever, and they have no rest day or night — the perpetual, restless punishment of all who worship the beast and receive his mark, set against the rest of those who die in the Lord. The verse displays the endless torment, framing the saints' patience and blessedness (vv.12-13).
12:
"Here is the patience of the saints: here are they that keep the commandments of God, and the faith of Jesus." The brief verse notes the patience of the saints who keep God's commandments. The grammatical voices note the application. Barnes and Robertson note the call to endurance drawn from the warning: "Here is the patience of the saints: here are they that keep the commandments of God, and the faith of Jesus" — in view of the warning of wrath against beast-worship (vv.9-11), the saints' patient endurance is called forth; the faithful are those who keep God's commandments and the faith of Jesus, enduring rather than yielding to the beast.

On "Here is the patience of the saints," the witnesses note the encouragement. Barnes and Ellicott note that "here is the patience (patient endurance) of the saints" — the dreadful fate of beast-worshippers (and the rest of the faithful, v.13) is the ground of the saints' patient endurance; they are sustained to endure the beast's persecution rather than receive his mark, knowing the contrast of destinies.

On "they that keep the commandments of God, and the faith of Jesus," the grammatical-historical note the marks of the saints: the faithful "keep the commandments of God, and the faith of Jesus" — the two marks of the true saint (as in 12:17): obedience to God's commandments and the holding of the faith of Jesus; these are they who, amid the beast's pressure, remain loyal to God and Christ. The verse displays the patience of the saints who keep God's commandments.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the application — "Here is the patience of the saints: here are they that keep the commandments of God, and the faith of Jesus," the saints' endurance called forth by the warning, the faithful keeping God's commandments and Christ's faith (Barnes, Robertson); "here is the patience of the saints" the contrast of destinies sustaining their endurance (Barnes, Ellicott); "keep the commandments of God, and the faith of Jesus," the two marks of the true saint (12:17, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the patient saints. The verse marks the patience: of the saints. The verse displays the marks: commandments and faith. The verse displays the endurance: amid the beast. The verse displays the loyalty: to God and Christ. The application displays the saints sustained to endure rather than yield. The verse displays the patience: called forth. The verse displays the commandments: kept. The verse displays the faith: of Jesus. The patience of the saints who keep God's commandments displays the lesson drawn from the warning: herein is the patient endurance of the saints — those who keep the commandments of God and the faith of Jesus, sustained to endure the beast's persecution rather than yield, in view of the contrast of destinies. The verse displays the enduring saints, blessed if they die in the Lord (v.13).
13:
"And I heard a voice from heaven saying unto me, Write, Blessed are the dead which die in the Lord from henceforth: Yea, saith the Spirit, that they may rest from their labours; and their works do follow them." The witnesses note the blessing on the dead who die in the Lord. On "Write, Blessed are the dead which die in the Lord from henceforth," the grammatical voices note the beatitude. Barnes and Robertson note the heavenly command and blessing: "Write, Blessed are the dead which die in the Lord from henceforth" — a voice from heaven bids John write a beatitude on the faithful dead; those who die "in the Lord" (in union with Christ, faithful unto death) are pronounced blessed; the comfort to the persecuted saints, that to die in the Lord is gain; "from henceforth" marking especially those who die under the beast's persecution.

On "that they may rest from their labours," the witnesses note the rest. Barnes and Henry note the Spirit's confirmation: "Yea, saith the Spirit, that they may rest from their labours" — the Spirit affirms the blessing; the faithful dead rest from their labors and sufferings; in pointed contrast to the beast-worshippers who have "no rest day nor night" (v.11), the saints rest; the toil and persecution are over, and rest begins.

On "their works do follow them," the puritan Henry notes the abiding fruit: "their works do follow them" — their good works accompany them, not as the ground of salvation but as its evidence and reward; the fruit of their faith follows them into the presence of God; what they did for Christ is not lost but follows them. The verse displays the blessing on the dead who die in the Lord.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the beatitude — "Blessed are the dead which die in the Lord from henceforth," the faithful dead pronounced blessed, the comfort to the persecuted (Barnes, Robertson); "that they may rest from their labours," the Spirit's confirmation, the saints' rest contrasting the beast-worshippers' restlessness (v.11, Barnes, Henry); "their works do follow them," the fruit of faith following them as evidence and reward (Henry). The verse displays the blessed dead. The verse marks the blessing: the dead in the Lord. The verse displays the Spirit: confirming. The verse displays the rest: from labors. The verse displays the works: following. The beatitude displays the faithful dead blessed, resting, their works following. The verse displays the dead: blessed. The verse displays the rest: granted. The verse displays the works: following. The blessing on the dead who die in the Lord displays the comfort set against the beast's terror: blessed are the dead who die in the Lord — they rest from their labors, says the Spirit, and their works follow them; the faithful, dying under persecution, enter blessed rest, their works their abiding fruit. The verse displays the blessed dead, before the harvest of the earth (vv.14-16).
14:
"And I looked, and behold a white cloud, and upon the cloud one sat like unto the Son of man, having on his head a golden crown, and in his hand a sharp sickle." The witnesses note the Son of man on the cloud with crown and sickle. On "behold a white cloud, and upon the cloud one sat like unto the Son of man," the grammatical voices note the figure of Christ. Barnes and Robertson note the appearance: "a white cloud, and upon the cloud one sat like unto the Son of man" — one like the Son of man (Christ, Daniel 7:13) is seen sitting on a white cloud; the figure of Christ coming to reap; the white cloud marking glory and the coming of the Lord (cf. 1:7; Matthew 24:30).

On "having on his head a golden crown," the witnesses note the kingly crown. Barnes and Ellicott note that the "golden crown (stephanos)" marks his royal authority and victory; the reaper is the crowned King, coming in his sovereignty to gather the harvest of the earth.

On "in his hand a sharp sickle," the puritan Henry and eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the instrument of harvest: "in his hand a sharp sickle" — the sickle is the instrument of reaping; Christ comes to reap the harvest of the earth; whether the harvest is the gathering of the elect or the judgment of the world (vv.15-16); the sharp sickle is ready for the appointed reaping. The verse displays the Son of man on the cloud with crown and sickle.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the figure — "a white cloud, and upon the cloud one sat like unto the Son of man," Christ on the white cloud, coming to reap (Daniel 7:13; 1:7, Barnes, Robertson); "a golden crown" his royal authority and victory (Barnes, Ellicott); "a sharp sickle" the instrument of the appointed harvest (Henry, Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the crowned reaper. The verse marks the cloud: white. The verse displays the figure: the Son of man. The verse displays the crown: golden. The verse displays the sickle: sharp. The vision displays Christ crowned and ready to reap. The verse displays the Son of man: enthroned on the cloud. The verse displays the crown: kingly. The verse displays the sickle: ready. The Son of man on the cloud with crown and sickle displays the coming of Christ to the harvest: one like the Son of man sits on a white cloud, crowned with gold and holding a sharp sickle — the crowned King come to reap the harvest of the earth. The verse displays the crowned reaper, bidden to thrust in the sickle (v.15).
15:
"And another angel came out of the temple, crying with a loud voice to him that sat on the cloud, Thrust in thy sickle, and reap: for the time is come for thee to reap; for the harvest of the earth is ripe." The brief verse notes the call to reap the ripe harvest. The grammatical voices note the summons. Barnes and Robertson note the angel's cry: "another angel came out of the temple, crying with a loud voice to him that sat on the cloud, Thrust in thy sickle, and reap" — an angel comes from the temple (from God's presence) and cries to the Son of man to thrust in the sickle and reap; the command for the harvest issues from the temple, by God's appointment.

On "the time is come for thee to reap," the witnesses note the appointed time. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the time is come for thee to reap" — the appointed hour of harvest has arrived; the reaping is at God's appointed time; the angel announces that the moment has come.

On "for the harvest of the earth is ripe," the grammatical-historical note the ripeness: "the harvest of the earth is ripe (dried, fully ripe)" — the harvest is ready; whether the ripening of the elect for gathering, or (with the vintage, vv.18-20) the ripening of the wicked for judgment; the harvest is ready to be reaped; the figure of the final ingathering/judgment at the appointed time. The verse displays the call to reap the ripe harvest.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the summons — "another angel came out of the temple, crying... Thrust in thy sickle, and reap," the angel from God's presence bidding the Son of man reap (Barnes, Robertson); "the time is come for thee to reap," the appointed hour of harvest arrived (Barnes, Ellicott); "the harvest of the earth is ripe," the harvest ready, the final ingathering/judgment (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the harvest-call. The verse marks the angel: from the temple. The verse displays the command: thrust in the sickle. The verse displays the time: come. The verse displays the harvest: ripe. The summons displays the appointed harvest commanded. The verse displays the sickle: to be thrust. The verse displays the time: arrived. The verse displays the harvest: ready. The call to reap the ripe harvest displays the command for the ingathering: an angel from the temple cries to the Son of man to thrust in his sickle and reap, for the time is come and the harvest of the earth is ripe — the final harvest commanded at God's appointed time. The verse displays the harvest-call, answered by the reaping (v.16).
16:
"And he that sat on the cloud thrust in his sickle on the earth; and the earth was reaped." The very brief verse notes the reaping of the earth. The grammatical voices note the harvest accomplished. Barnes and Robertson note the obedient reaping: "he that sat on the cloud thrust in his sickle on the earth; and the earth was reaped" — at the angel's word (v.15), the Son of man thrusts in his sickle, and the earth is reaped; the harvest is accomplished; the appointed ingathering is carried out swiftly and completely.

On "thrust in his sickle on the earth," the witnesses note the act. Barnes and Ellicott note that the Son of man "thrust in his sickle on the earth" — the reaping is done by Christ himself (or his agency); the figure of the decisive, appointed harvest of the earth at the end.

On "the earth was reaped," the grammatical-historical note the completion: "the earth was reaped" — the harvest is complete; whether the gathering of the elect (the wheat into the barn, Matthew 13:30) or the harvest of judgment, the reaping of the earth is finished at Christ's hand; the appointed ingathering accomplished. The verse displays the reaping of the earth.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the reaping — "he that sat on the cloud thrust in his sickle on the earth; and the earth was reaped," the Son of man reaping the earth at the appointed word (v.15, Barnes, Robertson); "thrust in his sickle on the earth," the decisive harvest by Christ (Barnes, Ellicott); "the earth was reaped," the appointed ingathering complete (Matthew 13:30, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the reaped earth. The verse marks the sickle: thrust in. The verse displays the reaping: of the earth. The verse displays the agent: the Son of man. The verse displays the completion: reaped. The act displays the appointed harvest accomplished. The verse displays the sickle: applied. The verse displays the earth: reaped. The verse displays the harvest: finished. The reaping of the earth displays the carrying out of the harvest: he who sat on the cloud thrust in his sickle, and the earth was reaped — the appointed ingathering of the earth accomplished at Christ's hand. The verse displays the reaped earth, followed by the vintage of wrath (vv.17-20).
17:
"And another angel came out of the temple which is in heaven, he also having a sharp sickle." The very brief verse notes the angel with a sharp sickle for the vintage. The grammatical voices note the second reaper. Barnes and Robertson note the new figure: "another angel came out of the temple which is in heaven, he also having a sharp sickle" — a second angel comes from the heavenly temple, also bearing a sharp sickle; this angel is to gather the vintage (vv.18-20), as the Son of man reaped the harvest (vv.14-16); the second reaping, of the grapes, is now introduced.

On "came out of the temple which is in heaven," the witnesses note the heavenly source. Barnes and Robertson note that the angel comes "out of the temple which is in heaven" — from God's presence; the command and the agency of the vintage proceed from God; the heavenly temple is the source of the appointed judgment.

On "he also having a sharp sickle," the grammatical-historical note the instrument: the angel "also" has "a sharp sickle" — like the Son of man (v.14), but now for the gathering of the grapes; the sharp sickle marks the readiness for the vintage of wrath; the instrument of the coming judgment on the wicked. The verse displays the angel with a sharp sickle for the vintage.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the second reaper — "another angel came out of the temple which is in heaven, he also having a sharp sickle," the second angel from the heavenly temple with a sickle for the vintage (Barnes, Robertson); "out of the temple which is in heaven," the heavenly source of the appointed judgment (Barnes, Robertson); "he also having a sharp sickle," the instrument for the vintage of wrath (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the vintage-angel. The verse marks the angel: from the temple. The verse displays the source: heaven. The verse displays the sickle: sharp. The verse displays the task: the vintage. The figure displays the angel ready for the vintage of judgment. The verse displays the angel: come. The verse displays the temple: heavenly. The verse displays the sickle: ready. The angel with a sharp sickle for the vintage displays the introduction of the second reaping: another angel comes from the heavenly temple, also bearing a sharp sickle — the appointed agent of the vintage of wrath. The verse displays the vintage-angel, bidden to gather the grapes (v.18).
18:
"And another angel came out from the altar, which had power over fire; and cried with a loud cry to him that had the sharp sickle, saying, Thrust in thy sharp sickle, and gather the clusters of the vine of the earth; for her grapes are fully ripe." The witnesses note the call to gather the ripe grapes of the earth. On "another angel came out from the altar, which had power over fire," the grammatical voices note the angel of the altar. Barnes and Robertson note the new herald: "another angel came out from the altar, which had power over fire" — an angel comes from the altar (the altar of incense, associated with the prayers of the saints, 6:9-10; 8:3-5), having power over fire; the call for the vintage comes from the altar, suggesting the judgment answers the prayers of the saints for vindication.

On "Thrust in thy sharp sickle, and gather the clusters of the vine of the earth," the witnesses note the command. Barnes and Ellicott note the cry: "Thrust in thy sharp sickle, and gather the clusters of the vine of the earth" — the angel of the altar bids the angel with the sickle gather the grape-clusters of the earth's vine; the "vine of the earth" is the wicked, ripe for judgment (in contrast to the true Vine, Christ); the gathering of the grapes for the winepress of wrath.

On "for her grapes are fully ripe," the grammatical-historical note the ripeness for judgment: "her grapes are fully ripe" — the wickedness of the earth is fully ripe, ready for judgment; the iniquity of the world has come to its full measure (cf. Genesis 15:16; Joel 3:13); the vintage of wrath is due. The verse displays the call to gather the ripe grapes of the earth.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the herald — "another angel came out from the altar, which had power over fire," the angel of the altar, the call answering the saints' prayers (6:9-10; 8:3-5, Barnes, Robertson); "gather the clusters of the vine of the earth," the gathering of the wicked, the vine of the earth, for judgment (Barnes, Ellicott); "her grapes are fully ripe," the earth's wickedness ripe for the vintage of wrath (Joel 3:13, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the vintage-call. The verse marks the angel: from the altar. The verse displays the power: over fire. The verse displays the command: gather the clusters. The verse displays the ripeness: fully ripe. The call displays the wicked gathered for the winepress of wrath. The verse displays the altar: source. The verse displays the grapes: the wicked. The verse displays the ripeness: complete. The call to gather the ripe grapes of the earth displays the command for the vintage of judgment: the angel of the altar, with power over fire, cries to thrust in the sickle and gather the clusters of the earth's vine, for her grapes are fully ripe — the wicked, ripe in iniquity, gathered for the winepress of God's wrath. The verse displays the gathered vintage, cast into the winepress (vv.19-20).
19:
"And the angel thrust in his sickle into the earth, and gathered the vine of the earth, and cast it into the great winepress of the wrath of God." The very brief verse notes the vintage cast into the winepress of wrath. The grammatical voices note the gathering and casting. Barnes and Robertson note the act of judgment: "the angel thrust in his sickle into the earth, and gathered the vine of the earth, and cast it into the great winepress of the wrath of God" — the angel reaps the earth's vine (the wicked) and casts it into the great winepress of God's wrath; the figure of the gathering of the wicked for the execution of judgment; the winepress, where the grapes are trodden, is the place of wrath.

On "gathered the vine of the earth," the witnesses note the gathering of the wicked. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the vine of the earth" — the wicked of the earth, ripe in iniquity — is gathered for judgment; the harvest of the ungodly, in contrast to the ingathering of the elect (v.16).

On "cast it into the great winepress of the wrath of God," the grammatical-historical note the winepress: the gathered vine is cast "into the great winepress of the wrath of God" — the winepress (Isaiah 63:1-6; Joel 3:13) is the figure of God's judgment, where the wicked are trodden as grapes; the "great winepress of the wrath of God" marks the severity and finality of the judgment on the ungodly. The verse displays the vintage cast into the winepress of wrath.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the act — "the angel thrust in his sickle into the earth, and gathered the vine of the earth, and cast it into the great winepress of the wrath of God," the wicked gathered and cast into the winepress of judgment (Barnes, Robertson); "the vine of the earth" the wicked ripe for judgment, in contrast to the elect (v.16, Barnes, Ellicott); "the great winepress of the wrath of God," the figure of God's severe judgment (Isaiah 63:3; Joel 3:13, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the winepress of wrath. The verse marks the sickle: thrust in. The verse displays the vine: gathered. The verse displays the casting: into the winepress. The verse displays the wrath: of God. The act displays the wicked gathered for the treading of judgment. The verse displays the vine: reaped. The verse displays the winepress: great. The verse displays the wrath: executed. The vintage cast into the winepress of wrath displays the execution of judgment on the wicked: the angel gathers the vine of the earth and casts it into the great winepress of the wrath of God — the ungodly gathered to be trodden in the winepress of judgment. The verse displays the winepress, trodden in blood (v.20).
20:
"And the winepress was trodden without the city, and blood came out of the winepress, even unto the horse bridles, by the space of a thousand and six hundred furlongs." The witnesses note the winepress trodden, blood to the horse bridles. On "the winepress was trodden without the city," the grammatical voices note the treading of judgment. Barnes and Robertson note the execution: "the winepress was trodden without (outside) the city" — the winepress of God's wrath is trodden outside the city; the treading (Isaiah 63:3) is the act of judgment, crushing the wicked as grapes; "without the city" perhaps marking the judgment as outside the holy city, or the place of execution.

On "blood came out of the winepress, even unto the horse bridles," the witnesses note the vast slaughter. Barnes and Ellicott note the figure of immense bloodshed: "blood came out of the winepress, even unto the horse bridles" — the treading produces blood (not wine) rising to the horses' bridles; the hyperbolic figure of an enormous, terrible slaughter; the vast extent of the judgment on the wicked.

On "by the space of a thousand and six hundred furlongs," the grammatical-historical note the extent: the blood flows "by the space of a thousand and six hundred furlongs (stadia, about 200 miles)" — the immense distance marks the vast, widespread extent of the judgment; whether a symbolic number (perhaps 4×4×100, the four quarters of the earth) or a figure for the whole land; the universality and magnitude of the judgment on the ungodly. The verse displays the winepress trodden, blood to the horse bridles.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the treading — "the winepress was trodden without the city," the winepress of wrath trodden, the wicked crushed in judgment (Isaiah 63:3, Barnes, Robertson); "blood came out of the winepress, even unto the horse bridles," the hyperbolic figure of an enormous, terrible slaughter (Barnes, Ellicott); "a thousand and six hundred furlongs," the vast extent of the judgment (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the trodden winepress. The verse marks the treading: without the city. The verse displays the blood: to the bridles. The verse displays the extent: 1600 furlongs. The verse displays the magnitude: vast slaughter. The judgment displays the wicked trodden in an immense slaughter. The verse displays the winepress: trodden. The verse displays the blood: deep. The verse displays the extent: widespread. The winepress trodden, blood to the horse bridles, displays the awful magnitude of the vintage of wrath: the winepress is trodden outside the city, and blood flows from it to the horses' bridles for a thousand six hundred furlongs — the figure of an enormous, widespread judgment, the wicked crushed in the winepress of God's wrath. The verse displays the trodden winepress, closing the harvest before the seven last plagues (ch.15).
